story:
Crime Scene Tours Ltd., run by Liz and Colin Stevenson, thrives on guiding visitors through sites of notorious murders, despite criticisms of bad taste. Their tours, popular with overseas tourists, employ actors and a touch of embellishment while steering clear of active cases and avoiding distress to victims’ families.

One July afternoon in the Bedfordshire countryside, a group that includes Bob and Grace Hansacker of Idaho stops at a pub by a lake before the next “on-site” segment. The Hansackers, practiced skeptics, are unimpressed by the trappings, and Colin’s brief appearance in a flashy car only confirms their cynicism. He promises they are about to experience the gruesome and grisly and then disappears back into the pub.

A young blonde guide introduces herself as Adele and announces a story of two lovers and a wrongful conviction. The group assumes she is an actress hired to heighten the mood. Adele leads them along the lake and toward a wood, asking them to keep to the path. She paints the scene: a warm evening in July 2005, a young woman walking alone from the pub toward the trees, perhaps to a secret meeting with her boyfriend—yet no one knows why she would enter the woods at dusk, alone. Adele gestures at the dark opening beneath the canopy with the practiced air of someone who can summon suspense out of thin air.

As they go deeper, the atmosphere changes: cooler, darker, mustier, the sun filtered to a sullen green. Bob asks whether anyone was ever arrested. Yes, Adele says: David Collins, the boyfriend. Witnesses saw them quarrel in the pub. The evidence, however, was circumstantial; he always insisted he was innocent. In prison, he hanged himself. Bob asks how the girl died. “We’ll get to that,” Adele replies, and presses on.

Adele suddenly leaves the path and wades through waist-high ferns, beckoning them after her. In a hush, she indicates a patch of ground. This, she says, is where the body was found: shallowly buried in leaf mould and bracken, unearthed days later by a dog. The attack was frenzied—sixty-two stab wounds to the face, neck, and torso. The group falls silent, chilled more by her flat, precise recital than by the shade.

A camera flash pops; several people yelp. From somewhere nearby comes a voice calling hello, and a middle-aged woman in a company badge, Deborah, crashes through the undergrowth. She looks aghast. Who told them to come out here? The group says Adele brought them. Deborah frowns. Adele? Is this a joke? Adele Jameson was the murdered girl. Gasps ripple through the party.

Bob, still convinced this is a stunt, applauds the “overacting.” To prove the point, he asks a Japanese tourist to show the picture he just took. On the camera’s screen is not a perky guide posing in the trees, but a young blonde woman half-hidden among the flattened ferns, twisted on her side where the undergrowth parts near the patch Adele had indicated, her features ruined and hair matted with dried blood. In that instant, it becomes clear that the company’s hired theatrics never extended this far: they had not been led by an employee at all, but by the very victim whose story they came to consume. The patch of earth Adele indicated lies quiet beneath a sudden shaft of sunlight, and she is gone.
